items.title: Verify the update sign and formula
items.avoid: Assuming the optimizer minimizes without checking loss trend after a few epochs.
items.prefer: Confirm θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) on a toy quadratic where the minimum is known analytically.
items.why: Sign flips and wrong loss aggregation are silent bugs that look like bad hyperparameters.
items.example: On L(w) = (w − 2)² from w = 0, η = 0.1, loss must drop monotonically; rising loss means addition or wrong gradient.

items.title: Monitor loss per epoch, not per mini-batch
items.avoid: Judging batch GD by noisy step-to-step metrics meant for SGD.
items.prefer: Log full-batch training loss once per epoch; expect smoother curves on convex or near-convex subproblems.
items.why: Batch GD has one meaningful update per pass; epoch-level loss reflects the algorithm you implemented.
items.example: Plot epoch vs L(θ); a sawtooth within an epoch signals accidental mini-batching, not true batch GD.

items.title: Start with a conservative learning rate
items.avoid: Copying large η values from mini-batch LLM recipes into full-batch toy runs.
items.prefer: Bracket η with a small value (e.g. 1e-3), confirm decrease, then increase until instability appears.
items.why: Full-batch gradients can have larger norm than mini-batch estimates; safe η depends on loss scale and curvature.
items.example: If epoch loss spikes after raising η from 0.01 to 0.5, back off to the last stable value.

items.title: Know when to leave batch GD
items.avoid: Insisting on full-dataset passes when data no longer fits in memory or time budget.
items.prefer: Switch to mini-batch SGD (same update rule, subset gradients) for large-scale Gen AI training.
items.why: Batch GD is a pedagogical and small-data tool; scale demands stochastic estimates, not a different update formula.
items.example: 10M-token corpus fine-tuning should use mini-batches; batch GD is for prototypes on thousands of examples.
